chapters.title: How pressure shapes people
chapters.text:
Under stress, people act like one of two animals. Some freeze in place like a kitten and wait for rescue. Others, like a baby monkey, run toward a caregiver and hold on tight. The tools in this book help people who already have the monkey mindset, and they can also help kittens switch into a more active mode.

Your creative edge comes from being active and positive about yourself.

Your attitude is a choice you can make. We cannot choose our parents or the day we will die, but we can choose how to live between those two points. Creative people are not just acted upon. They choose to be the subject of their own lives. They pick I over me.

The tools in this summary will push you to see the world from a new angle. Where you once saw one path or none, you will start to see many.

You will look at the same information as everyone else and notice something new.

Michael Michalko wrote this book to share his methods. The tools fall into two broad camps, linear and intuitive. Linear tools help you rework existing ideas into fresh angles. Intuitive tools tap your imagination to spark new ones.

What does this knowledge give you? It helps you generate new ways to make money, design business solutions, improve existing ideas, become more valuable to the people around you, refresh old industries, and lift productivity.

Learning the techniques is not enough. You have to use them. That is why you need the monkey mindset. If you are stuck in kitten mode, the rest of this book will show you how to switch. The techniques are simple and they work.

chapters.title: Creative growth starts with killing self doubt
chapters.text:
A creative mind has to push past fears, uncertainties, and doubts, or what the author calls FUDS. Admit your FUDS, name them, and let your mind look for replacements. The shift comes from two simple beliefs. You are capable of change. You are no less talented than anyone else.

Use the tick tock exercise to take on your FUDS. Draw a table with two columns. Label the left tick and the right tock. Under tick, write the negative thought that feeds your doubt. Under tock, write a positive line that cancels out the negative.

After reframing, follow up with self affirmation and creative affirmation. Self affirmation recalls your past wins and your good qualities to fuel new wins. Creative affirmation is the belief that you are creative. Once you see yourself as a winner, a thinker, and a solver, you will act like one.

Write your affirmations about being creative for five days in a row.

Set an idea quota for yourself to move from a kitten to a monkey.

An idea quota is a simple exercise. Give yourself a target number of ideas to come up with every day.

Pay attention to the world around you. You will find things that push you toward new ground. Step away from your routine now and then to force new thinking. Feed your mind by reading and by thinking about what you read. Build a brain bank, a place to store ideas for later. Travel exposes you to new eyes and new insights. When an idea shows up, write it down right away. Listing ideas as they come sharpens your thinking. Flexible thinking comes from seeing beyond the conventional answer.

Now that you have moved from kitten to monkey, how do the thinkertoys help you tinker with a problem until it gives way?

A small note worth knowing. Thomas Edison held over a thousand patents in his lifetime.

chapters.title: Turn problems into openings
chapters.text:
Coming up with ideas without a clear target is a waste of effort. List the problems you want to solve. Ask what you gain by solving each one. The gain may be money, emotion, attitude, or skill. Otto Frederick Rohwedder, for example, spent sixteen years building a bread slicing machine because he did not enjoy slicing bread himself. He never got rich from it. His gain was the simple pleasure of pre sliced bread.

Once you are clear on the benefit, take the challenge and start generating ideas. The number of ideas you can put on the table sets the pace at which you reach a fresh answer. The tools you pick also depend on how you framed the problem.

Questions help you look at a problem from many sides until you find the real issue to tackle.

You can reframe a problem by swapping words for their synonyms or by writing questions that open the mind. Ask, for example, what new funding sources could support this project. Look at the problem up close and from a distance. Break it down into smaller pieces and tackle each one on its own.

A small note worth knowing. By 1933, about eighty percent of bread sold in the United States came pre sliced.

chapters.title: Use both sides of the brain for fresh solutions
chapters.text:
A single point of view draws imaginary lines around the problem and kills fresh thinking. Each of us leans on one side of the brain more than the other, left or right.

Single points of view often come from ignoring the side of the brain you use less.

Left brained thinkers tend to follow steps and stick to what is in front of them. Right brained thinkers tend to see the whole picture and pull ideas out of thin air.

Statisticians, researchers, and writers lean left. Musicians, composers, and other creatives lean right. The thinkertoys help you bring both sides online. Linear thinkertoys suit ordered or sequential problems. Intuitive thinkertoys suit big picture thinking or new lines of thought. To get the most from the tools, use one at a time, alternate between linear and intuitive, and pick the tool randomly when you face a fresh challenge.

But do not chase every idea the tools throw at you, or you will be pulled in too many directions. Collect and write down your ideas. Then judge them against a standard that fits the kind of suggestions on the table. The ideas that pass become possible solutions.

If you are ready to start, begin with the first set of linear thinkertoys.

chapters.title: Rearrange the ideas you already have
chapters.text:
The thinkertoys that help you see a challenge from a new angle include False Faces, Slice and Dice, Cherry Split, Think Bubbles, and Scamper.

False Faces is about assumptions that pretend to be truth. When you face a challenge, write down everything you assume about it. Then write the opposite of each assumption. Ask how you would make the opposite come true, and pick one to try.

Theories stay unchallenged until someone proves them wrong.

Slice and Dice lists the parts of an item so you can improve each one. Car makers use this tool to design new models. They list the parts of a car and study how each feature can be made better.

Cherry Split splits the challenge into two big pieces and treats each one on its own until a new idea hides inside one of them. Start by splitting the problem in two. Pick one side and list its features. Study each detail until you find something you can change.

Think Bubbles is a mind map. It shows how the pieces of a challenge connect. A mind map gives you a picture of how your brain links information. It is easier to move things around on a map than to shuffle them in your head.

Scamper pushes you to ask questions until a new answer appears. If you want to be a better public speaker, Scamper can help. Ask yourself what strategy you could swap in for your current style, how you could combine your style with another, what you can borrow from a speaker you admire, how you could change your current style, how you could make parts of it stronger, what habits you need to drop, and how you could rearrange the whole thing.

You can use Scamper on almost any problem.

chapters.title: Look inside for answers
chapters.text:
Many answers sit just below the surface of the mind. To bring them up, try these intuitive tools. Chilling Out, Blue Roses, The Three Bs, Rattlesnakes and Roses, and Stone Soup.

Chilling Out relaxes the mind and body until you slip into a deep, calm state. In that mode, ideas buried in your subconscious rise to the surface.

Blue Roses is about intuition. Intuition means listening to how you feel about a situation and using that feeling to choose your next move.

You can train your intuition by making quick guesses about everyday life.

When your phone rings, try to guess who is calling. Use your intuitive skills whenever it makes sense, and combine them when you can.

The Three Bs stand for bus, bed, and bath. This tool uses the power of incubation. Ideas show up when you are not trying. Instead of staring at the problem, do something else. While your mind is busy elsewhere, a strong idea can rise on its own. Get clear on the problem, gather the facts, let your brain register the need to solve it, walk away for a set amount of time, and when you return, you may find the answer waiting.

Rattlesnakes and Roses plays with the flow of ideas. New ideas come from comparing things from very different fields and finding the bridges between them. Pick a keyword from the problem you want to solve, pick an unrelated field you know something about, list the images you associate with that field, and look for ties between your keyword and that list.

Our imagination is one of the things that sets us apart from every other creature.

Stone Soup is the what if tool. In this mode, do not judge any idea that comes up. Just ask what the world would look like if the idea were true. Big leaps of imagination can lead to fresh answers for old problems.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
The human mind holds a treasury of ideas that can change the world. Anyone can think like a creative genius under the right conditions. The trouble is that most of us have been trained to think in narrow, familiar ways, and that habit buries our originality deep in the subconscious. The thinkertoys dive into that sea of ideas and bring them up into the light.

The tools come in two flavors. Linear thinkertoys help you improve a concept or theory that already exists. Intuitive thinkertoys help you create value where none existed before. Used together, the tools wake up both the analytical and the creative sides of the brain.

Do not assume the best idea cannot be made better. Progress depends on people who keep inventing and reinventing. You have to feel a steady itch with the best plan in front of you in order to build something better.

There are no good or bad events in themselves. Events take on the meaning we give them. Train your mind to read events from many angles and never lock yourself into a single view. Once your mind can see many possibilities, you will not be shaken by passing trouble.

If you want to become a solver, switch your self image from a kitten to a monkey through daily affirmations. Learn the tools in this summary and put them to work.

Try this. The first move toward a creative mind is to take on your fears, uncertainties, and doubts. Use the tick tock exercise. Draw a table with two columns. Label one tick and the other tock. Under tick, write down the negative thought, fear, or doubt. Look at it carefully. Under tock, write the positive version of that thought.

You made it to the end. Well done.
